dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 90 responding for chest pain.
Hi, I'm Ali, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
Ma'am, I'm Derek, and I'll be working with Ali today.
Chest pain starting yesterday. It's worse today and goes down my left arm and into my back.
He also has a fever of about 101 degrees Fahrenheit.
Ma'am, can you tell me your name?
My name is Marie.
What city and county are we in?
We're in Richmond, Chesterfield County.
What is today's date?
I think it's October 14th.
Can you tell me what happened today?
I woke up with chest pain and it just got worse.
Patient is conscious, alert, and oriented to person, place, time, and event.
We are going to do a couple of things all at once. Do you mind?
No.
Your airway is patent and clear.
Airway is patent and self-maintained.

dialogue.micro_intent: check_breathing - Rate and depth of respirations
dialogue.utterance: Let me listen to your breathing for a moment.

dialogue.micro_intent: check_breathing - Rate and depth of respirations
dialogue.utterance: Respirations are at 22 breaths per minute.

dialogue.micro_intent: check_breathing - Oxygen saturation measured with pulse oximetry
dialogue.utterance: SpO₂ is 98%.
Heart rate is 103 beats per minute, regular and strong with a palpable radial pulse.
Let me check your skin.
Your skin is cool, pale, and dry. It doesn't seem diaphoretic.
Blood pressure is 108 over 71.
Ma'am, your Glasgow Coma Scale is 15 across eye, verbal, and motor.
When did the chest pain first start?
Yesterday morning, very slowly, but it is much worse today.
Has any activity made the pain worse?
No, it's just there constantly.
Can you describe the pain? Is it sharp, dull, pressure?
It's described as a pressure like angina, centered in my chest.
Does it go anywhere else?
Yes, down my left arm and into my back between the shoulder blades.
You said 7 earlier. Has it changed at all?
A little less now, maybe a 6 or 6.5.
Has it been constant or on and off?
It's pretty much constant.
Ma'am, what other symptoms are you experiencing?
She's had a fever too. She couldn't sleep much.
Do you have any known allergies?
No.
Are you taking any medications currently?
Yes, Calcium, Oxycodone (Percocet), Vitamin D, and Zofran.
Do you have any past medical conditions or surgeries?
I have Non-Hodgkin's lymphoma.

dialogue.micro_intent: S.A.M.P.L.E.- events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: What caused you to call 911 today?

dialogue.micro_intent: S.A.M.P.L.E.- events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: She called because her chest pain got significantly worse this morning.
You mentioned having lymphoma. Is there a port or anything in place for chemotherapy treatments?
Yes, I have a central port on my chest for chemo, but it's not for this situation.
Ma'am, we need to check your blood sugar now. It will be a quick prick on the fingertip. Ready?
Okay, your blood sugar is reading 0. We'll continue to monitor this closely.
Pulse remains at 103 and strong.
Respirations are still at 22. Let me reassess in a moment.
Blood pressure is currently 108 over 71 millimeters of mercury.
SpO₂ is 98% and stable at this time.
We are now placing leads for the 4-lead ECG. It will be on for monitoring only while we complete this exam. Ready?
Yes.
We have started 4-lead ECG monitoring on the patient per protocol for chest pain assessment. Connecting to EKG device now.
12-lead ECG is now applied for baseline tracing. We have a sinus rhythm at this time with some considerations in lead III due to prior assessment.
Reviewing EKG tracing, a normal sinus rhythm is confirmed for the patient. We will monitor this during transport and pending further EKG assessments at the hospital.
We're now attempting to start a 22-gauge IV in your left arm to establish venous access. Ready?.
First IV attempt unsuccessful. Repositioning and attempting in the other hand now.

Second IV attempt unsuccessful. We've established that venous access in the extremities is not available at this time. We will continue to support with other treatment modalities and repeat the 12-lead ECG for cardiac confirmation.
Per standing order for suspected cardiac chest pain, we are now administering 324 mg of Aspirin oral to help reduce potential cardiac inflammation. Is this okay with you?
Yes, that's fine.
Rechecking respirations now, they are at 20 breaths per minute and still not labored.
Pulse has slowed to 76 beats per minute, still with a strong, regular rhythm and easily palpable radial pulse.
Blood pressure is now 111 over 71. It seems stable at this point.
SpO₂ is currently 93 percent.
We have just conducted a second 12-lead ECG tracing. Evaluating the results now - it's showing a normal sinus rhythm for a male over 40.
All current cardiac vitals remain stable and within normal EKG findings per 12-lead traceovers.
Ma'am, based on our protocol for chest pain with suspected cardiac involvement, we are transporting you to VCU Medical Center for further evaluation. Is this acceptable?
That's fine with me.
We will now assist you into the cot and move you for transport safely in a position of comfort.
We are deploying the stair chair with two EMS members assisting you to the ground.
Straps are now in place to secure the patient. You're stable for transport.
Patient is now safely transferred to the cot.
You are secure, ready for transport to the hospital.
We are now arriving at VCU Health emergency department.
Patient is being gently transferred from the ambulance cot to the hospital bed by sheet pull using three attendants.
Patient is now secured to the hospital bed, and we're finalizing the transport handoff.
Full patient report has been given to receiving hospital staff.
We have called ahead with full clinical findings and protocol rationale for the receiving hospital via personal cell phone as we approached the facility.
Patient is signed in with hospital medical staff and is preparing for further care as needed.
